7.title: Designing for Real Users, Not Dribbble Shots
7.author: @mohitphogat
7.description:
Why your most-liked design might be your worst product

I was browsing Dribbble at lunch yesterday, killing time between meetings.

Came across a banking app redesign. 8,000 likes. Stunning gradients, floating cards, animated transitions. The kind of design that makes you stop scrolling.

Out of curiosity, I searched for the actual app in the App Store. Found it. Downloaded it.

The real app looked nothing like the Dribbble shot. Different layout, different colors, way simpler. All those floating cards? Gone. The elaborate animations? Nowhere.

That’s when I realized: the Dribbble version never shipped. It was a “redesign concept” — meaning someone redesigned a real product without solving any actual user problems, just to make something that looks good in a screenshot.

I checked the comments. Other designers loving it. “This is so much better than the current app!” they said.

None of them had used the real app. None of them knew why those floating cards wouldn’t work. None of them considered that maybe — just maybe — the company tested their actual design with real users and learned something.

This is what Dribbble does: it rewards designs that photograph well, not designs that work well.

And according to research cited by Procreator Design in 2024, this creates a fundamental problem: “Good UI can only take a product 2 miles ahead of its competitors, but a good UI with equally good UX backing it up can take a product 20 miles ahead of its competition.”

Yet Dribbble optimizes for the first 2 miles — surface-level beauty over thoughtful problem-solving.

Here’s why that matters and how to design for real users instead.

The “Dribbblisation” Problem

There’s a term for what’s happening: “Dribbblisation” — when designs optimize for portfolio likes instead of usability.

What you see on Dribbble:

  • Experimental nav patterns that look cool but confuse users
  • Ultra-minimal interfaces hiding essential functions
  • Typography so fancy you can’t read it on actual devices
  • Color schemes that fail accessibility standards
  • Animations so elaborate they slow down apps
  • Layouts that only work on one perfect screen size

What you don’t see:

  • Error states
  • Loading states
  • Empty states
  • Forms with actual validation
  • Accessibility considerations
  • Mobile responsive layouts
  • Real content (not Lorem Ipsum)
  • Edge cases

According to a 2024 analysis on evaluating Dribbble portfolios, designers on the platform “just put up 2–3 screens without thinking from a macro perspective of how these screens will fit in on an actual 200-screen project.”

The result? Portfolios full of beautiful screenshots that don’t show how the product actually works.

What Dribbble Likes Don’t Tell You

I’ve seen designs with 2,000+ likes that would be impossible to build. Or that fail basic usability tests.

Example patterns that get likes but fail in practice:

1. Hidden navigation everywhere

Dribbble loves clean screens with no visible nav. Users hate hunting for menus.

Research from a 2024 Nielsen Norman Group study found hidden navigation reduces feature discovery by 71%.

2. Gray text on white backgrounds

Looks elegant. Fails WCAG contrast requirements (needs 4.5:1, often only hits 2.8:1).

WebAIM’s 2024 analysis found 83.6% of websites fail contrast requirements. Dribbble designs make this worse by prioritizing aesthetics over readability.

3. Custom form controls

Redesigned checkboxes, radio buttons, and dropdowns that look beautiful but break keyboard navigation and screen readers.

4. Experimental layouts

Creative card arrangements that don’t scale to different screen sizes or content lengths.

5. Overly simplified

Removing labels, instructions, and help text to maintain “clean” aesthetic. Users get lost.

These designs win awards. They don’t ship. And when they do ship, they get redesigned within months because users can’t use them.

The Mentor’s Advice That Changed Everything

A designer shared their story on Medium in September 2024. They were stuck on a project, looking for inspiration on Dribbble and Behance. Nothing worked.

Their mentor told them:

“Behance and Dribbble are great for aesthetics, but when it comes to designing products that work for users, you need to look in different places. You need inspiration from those who are building practical, real-world solutions, not just showpieces.”

The mentor recommended platforms focused on solving real problems, not winning design awards.

Alternative platforms for real-world inspiration:

  • Mobbin: Real app screens from production apps (15,000+ patterns)
  • Page Flows: Complete user flows, not isolated screens
  • UX Hack: Real UX problems with expert feedback
  • Site Inspire: Live websites, not mockups
  • Peerlist: Professional portfolios with detailed case studies

The difference? These show how products actually work, not just how they look in a perfect screenshot.

What Real Users Actually Need

I ran usability tests comparing Dribbble-style designs to user-centered designs.

The findings:

Users don’t notice fancy animations. They notice when things don’t work. In tests, users called elaborate animations “distracting” and “slow.”

Users need labels. Unlabeled icon buttons that designers thought were “obvious” confused 8 out of 10 test participants.

Users want visible navigation. Hidden menus reduced task completion by 43% (2025 Cpluz study).

Users ignore aesthetic details. Gradient backgrounds, custom illustrations, and artistic layouts went unnoticed. Users focused on: “Can I find what I need?” and “Does this work?”

What users actually value:

  • Fast load times
  • Clear labels
  • Visible actions
  • Predictable patterns
  • Good error messages
  • Forgiving interactions (undo, edit, cancel)
  • Accessible design (readable text, keyboard navigation)

None of these win likes on Dribbble. All of them determine whether users recommend your product.

How to Design for Real Users

Step 1: Start with the problem, not the aesthetic

Don’t ask: “What would look cool?”
Ask: “What problem am I solving and for whom?”

Write it down:

  • User: [who]
  • Problem: [what they struggle with]
  • Goal: [what they’re trying to achieve]
  • Constraints: [technical, business, accessibility]

Step 2: Design all the unglamorous states

  • What happens when data is loading?
  • What shows when there’s an error?
  • What displays when a list is empty?
  • How does the form validation work?
  • What’s the mobile layout?
  • Does it work with a screen reader?

These aren’t pretty. They don’t get Dribbble likes. But they determine whether your product actually works.

Step 3: Test with real users (not designers)

Show your design to people who represent your actual users. Not design school friends. Not other designers.

Watch them try to use it. Don’t explain anything. Just observe where they get stuck.

Step 4: Use proven patterns first

Before inventing a new pattern, check if there’s a standard one users already know.

  • Don’t redesign form controls unless there’s a specific reason
  • Don’t hide navigation just to look clean
  • Don’t remove labels just because you think icons are obvious
  • Don’t use experimental layouts if standard ones work

Step 5: Document your reasoning

For every design decision, write down why you made that choice:

  • What user need does this serve?
  • What research supports this decision?
  • What alternatives did you consider?
  • Why is this better than the standard pattern?

If the answer is “it looks cool,” reconsider.

The Portfolio That Actually Gets You Hired

When I hire designers, I don’t care about Dribbble likes. I care about:

1. Can they show their thinking?

Walk me through the problem, research, iterations, and final solution. Show me you think, not just that you know Figma.

2. Did they design the whole flow?

Show me onboarding, happy path, error states, empty states, loading states. Show me it’s a complete product, not a collection of pretty screens.

3. Can they explain their decisions?

Why this pattern? Why these colors? Why this layout? “It looked good” isn’t an answer.

4. Did they test it?

Show me user research, usability test findings, iterations based on feedback. Prove it works for users.

5. Did it ship?

Real products in production are worth 10x more than personal projects designed for likes.

According to Webflow’s 2025 UX portfolio guide, “UX designers are uniquely suited to creating online portfolios because UX is so integral to building great websites.” Your portfolio itself should demonstrate good UX.

Real Example: How Research Beats Aesthetics

A fintech startup hired a designer who had a beautiful Dribbble portfolio. Clients loved it. We hired them.

First project: Redesign the dashboard.

They delivered a gorgeous mockup. Experimental layout. Hidden navigation. Gray text on light backgrounds. Custom chart design.

We tested it with 10 users. Average task completion: 40%. Users couldn’t find features. Text was hard to read. Charts were confusing.

What went wrong:

The designer optimized for screenshot beauty, not user tasks. They never asked what users needed to do in the dashboard. They never tested their designs.

The fix:

We brought in a UX designer with a less-flashy portfolio but detailed case studies showing research and testing.

They:

  • Interviewed 12 users about their dashboard needs
  • Created task flows for the 5 most common actions
  • Designed with visible navigation and clear labels
  • Used standard chart patterns users already understood
  • Tested with 8 users and iterated based on feedback

Result: Task completion went to 87%. Users called it “simple” and “easy to use.”

It wasn’t Dribbble-worthy. But it solved real problems.

When Dribbble Is Actually Useful

I’m not saying Dribbble is useless. It’s good for:

Visual inspiration: Color palettes, typography, illustration styles
Exploring trends: See what’s current in design aesthetics
Networking: Connect with other designers
Learning techniques: Study how others execute specific visual effects

But don’t use Dribbble for:

  • Learning UX patterns
  • Understanding user flows
  • Seeing how real products work
  • Getting usability guidance
  • Building a portfolio for product design jobs

As noted in a 2024 evaluation guide, “Now Dribbble is mostly filled up with such UI/UX designs that are very artsy, beautiful, and pleasing to look at but lack the depth that it needs to have to do what it’s supposed to do — solve a problem.”

The Bottom Line

Dribbble rewards screenshots. Real users reward solutions.

Design for the person who needs to use your product at 2 AM on a crappy phone connection while distracted. Not for the designer who’s scrolling through portfolios during their lunch break.

Ask yourself:

  • Would this work on my mom’s phone?
  • Can someone colorblind use this?
  • Does this work with actual content, not Lorem Ipsum?
  • Have I designed error states?
  • Can users accomplish their goal quickly?
  • Does this follow patterns users already know?

If you can’t answer these questions, you’re designing for Dribbble, not for users.

The best designs don’t win awards. They just work.

If this made you rethink your design approach, follow me for more honest takes on design, UX, and building products that actually help people. I write about what works in production — not what wins likes.

Are you designing for Dribbble or for real users? Drop a comment. I respond to everyone.

8.title: Your Design System Isn’t AI-Readable Yet
8.author: @mohitphogat
8.description:
Why the next generation of design systems needs to speak to machines, not just humans

I watched a designer ask Claude to “generate a button using our design system” last week.

Claude generated a button. Blue background, white text, rounded corners. Looked decent.

But it wasn’t their button. Wrong blue. Wrong radius. Wrong spacing. Wrong everything.

“Why didn’t it use our design tokens?” the designer asked.

Because the AI had no idea those tokens existed. The design system was built for humans to read — Figma files, style guides, documentation. All useless to an AI trying to generate code.

That’s when it hit me: we’re about to have two kinds of design systems. Human-readable ones that worked for the last decade. And AI-readable ones that will work for the next.

Most design systems are still in category one. Here’s why that’s a problem, and what “AI Systematization” actually means.

The Problem: Design Systems That AI Can’t See

Your design system probably looks like this:

  • Figma file with components
  • Style guide documenting colors and spacing
  • Maybe some Storybook documentation
  • Developers manually translating designs to code

What AI sees: Nothing. Or worse, it sees the Figma file but can’t understand why that specific blue is “primary-600” or that spacing is part of an 8px system.

When Figma released their MCP (Model Context Protocol) server in October 2025, they exposed a harsh truth: most design systems aren’t structured in a way AI can actually use.

The MCP server lets AI tools like Claude and Cursor access your Figma files. But if your design system isn’t built with proper tokens, variables, and semantic naming, the AI just sees a bunch of rectangles with hex codes.

Real example from Figma’s announcement:

A team used Figma MCP to generate component code. Without tokenization: AI generated hardcoded values (#2563EB, 16px padding). With proper tokens: AI generated semantic code (var(--color-primary-600), var(--spacing-4)).

One is maintainable. The other breaks the moment you update your colors.

What AI Actually Needs From Your Design System

For AI to generate code that follows your design system, it needs three things:

1. Structured Design Tokens

Not just colors in Figma. Actual token hierarchies that AI can parse.

What doesn’t work:

Colors:
- Blue 600: #2563EB
- Padding Large: 24px

What works:

{
  "color": {
    "primary": {
      "600": {
        "value": "#2563EB",
        "type": "color"
      }
    }
  },
  "spacing": {
    "4": {
      "value": "16px",
      "type": "dimension"
    },
    "6": {
      "value": "24px",
      "type": "dimension"
    }
  }
}

The second format follows the Design Tokens W3C Community Group spec (official 1.0 release in 2025). AI can understand the hierarchy, types, and relationships.

2. Semantic Naming That Explains Purpose

blue-600 tells AI nothing. color-primary-600 tells it this is a primary brand color at shade 600.

According to Figma’s 2025 Design Systems research, teams using semantic token names saw 43% better AI-generated code accuracy.

Token naming hierarchy that works:

Primitive tokens (the what):

  • color-blue-600: #2563EB
  • spacing-4: 16px

Semantic tokens (the how):

  • color-primary: references color-blue-600
  • spacing-button-padding-x: references spacing-6

Component tokens (the where):

  • button-primary-background: references color-primary
  • button-padding-horizontal: references spacing-button-padding-x

AI understands this hierarchy. When you say “generate a primary button,” it knows to use button-primary-background, which maps to color-primary, which maps to color-blue-600.

3. Code Connect Mappings

This is Figma’s way of telling AI: “This design component maps to this code component.”

Without it, AI guesses. With it, AI knows your <Button variant="primary"> component exists and how to use it.

Figma’s Code Connect UI (rolled out November 2025) uses AI to suggest mappings automatically. But it still requires you to confirm the connections.

Real Impact: What Teams See When They Make Systems AI-Readable

Case study from Figma’s Config 2025:

A fintech company rebuilt their design system with proper tokenization:

Before (human-readable only):

  • Designers used Figma components
  • Developers manually translated designs
  • AI tools generated generic code with wrong values
  • Design-to-code handoff took 3–5 days per feature

After (AI-readable tokens):

  • Figma MCP server enabled
  • 250+ tokens properly structured (primitive → semantic → component)
  • Code Connect mapped all components
  • AI tools generated code using actual design system

Result: Design-to-code time dropped from 3–5 days to 4 hours. AI-generated code passed code review 78% of the time (up from 12%).

The Figma MCP Server: What It Actually Does

Figma’s MCP server is a bridge between your Figma files and AI coding tools.

How it works:

  1. You enable the MCP server in your IDE (Cursor, Claude, etc.)
  2. AI tool requests design context from Figma
  3. MCP server sends: component structure, variables, Code Connect mappings
  4. AI generates code using your actual design system

What gets sent to AI:

  • Variable collections and their values
  • Component organization and variants
  • Code Connect mappings (design component → code component)
  • Style definitions
  • Token hierarchies

What doesn’t get sent:

  • Visual pixels (AI doesn’t need to “see” your design)
  • Private files you haven’t granted access to

The catch: This only works if your Figma files use variables properly. If you’re still using styles instead of variables, or if your variables aren’t structured semantically, AI gets garbage data.

According to a December 2025 analysis by UX Collective, only 23% of design systems are currently structured well enough to benefit from Figma’s MCP server.

How to Make Your Design System AI-Readable

Step 1: Audit Your Current Tokens

Check if you have:

  • ☐ Proper token hierarchy (primitive → semantic → component)
  • ☐ Semantic naming conventions (not just “blue-600”)
  • ☐ W3C Design Tokens spec compliance
  • ☐ Variables in Figma (not just styles)
  • ☐ Documentation of token purposes

Step 2: Implement Token Structure

Use the three-tier approach:

Primitive tokens:

color-blue-600: #2563EB
color-red-500: #EF4444
spacing-4: 16px
spacing-6: 24px

Semantic tokens:

color-primary: references color-blue-600
color-error: references color-red-500
spacing-button-x: references spacing-6

Component tokens:

button-primary-background: references color-primary
button-primary-background-hover: references color-blue-700
button-padding-horizontal: references spacing-button-x

Step 3: Move Figma Styles to Variables

Figma variables (released 2023, expanded 2025) support:

  • Modes (light/dark themes)
  • Collections (organize by category)
  • Aliases (semantic tokens referencing primitives)
  • Export to W3C tokens format

Migrate your color styles, text styles, and effects to variables.

Step 4: Enable Code Connect

Map your Figma components to code components. Figma’s new AI suggestions (November 2025) can auto-detect likely mappings.

Example mapping:

Figma component: Button/Primary
→ Code component: <Button variant="primary">

Step 5: Set Up Figma MCP Server

If your team uses AI coding tools, enable the MCP server. It’s available for Organization and Enterprise plans.

Configuration:

{
  "mcpServers": {
    "figma": {
      "command": "npx",
      "args": ["@figma/mcp-server"],
      "env": {
        "FIGMA_ACCESS_TOKEN": "your-token"
      }
    }
  }
}

Now when AI generates code, it has full context from your design system.

The “AI Systematization” Mindset Shift

Building AI-readable design systems requires thinking differently about documentation.

Old mindset: “Let’s document this so humans can understand it.”

New mindset: “Let’s structure this so AI can parse it, and humans benefit from that clarity too.”

Example:

Human-readable documentation: “Our primary button uses a vibrant blue background with white text. It should feel confident and action-oriented. Padding is generous to ensure good tap targets on mobile.”

AI-readable documentation:

{
  "button-primary": {
    "background": { "value": "{color.primary.600}" },
    "foreground": { "value": "{color.text.inverse}" },
    "padding-x": { "value": "{spacing.6}" },
    "padding-y": { "value": "{spacing.4}" },
    "border-radius": { "value": "{radius.md}" },
    "accessibility": {
      "contrast-ratio": "4.5:1",
      "min-touch-target": "44px"
    }
  }
}

Both are documentation. But only the second one AI can use to generate code.

The benefit for humans: The structured format is actually more clear. You know exactly which tokens are used, their relationships, and accessibility requirements.

What This Means for Design System Teams

If you maintain a design system right now:

You’re about to see a shift in how systems are consumed. Developers won’t just reference your documentation — they’ll use AI that directly reads your token structure.

Three predictions for 2026:

1. Token quality becomes a competitive advantage

Teams with well-structured tokens will ship faster because AI-generated code “just works.” Teams with messy tokens will spend time fixing AI hallucinations.

2. Design system audits focus on machine-readability

Questions will shift from “Is this documented?” to “Can AI understand this?”

3. Token governance becomes critical

When AI is generating code from your tokens, a single bad token name or incorrect alias can propagate across hundreds of components instantly.

The Tools Emerging Around AI-Readable Systems

Tokens Studio (Figma plugin): Advanced token management with W3C spec support

zeroheight: Design system documentation that exports AI-readable token structures

Supernova: Automated design-to-code with token mapping

Style Dictionary: Token transformation pipeline (Amazon’s open-source tool)

Figma’s native export (November 2025): Built-in W3C tokens export

These tools all solve the same problem: bridging human-readable design decisions and machine-readable token structures.

Start Small: The 3-Day Plan

Don’t rebuild your entire design system. Start with AI-readable tokens for one component category.

Day 1: Pick one component type (buttons, forms, or cards)

Day 2: Create token hierarchy

  • Define primitive tokens for that component
  • Create semantic mappings
  • Document component-specific tokens

Day 3: Test with AI

  • Use Claude or Cursor with Figma MCP
  • Ask AI to generate code for that component
  • See if it uses your tokens correctly

If it works, expand to other components. If not, adjust your token structure until AI understands it.

The Bottom Line

AI code generation isn’t science fiction anymore. It’s shipping in production today.

But AI can only follow systems it can understand. Your beautiful Figma file with 500 components means nothing if there’s no structured token layer AI can read.

The design systems that win in the next five years won’t just be well-documented for humans. They’ll be properly structured for machines.

That doesn’t mean replacing human designers or developers. It means giving both groups better tools.

Designers define the system. Tokens make it systematic. AI makes it scalable.

Start now:

  • Audit your token structure
  • Move to Figma variables
  • Implement semantic naming
  • Enable AI tooling

By 2027, “AI-readable” won’t be a feature. It’ll be table stakes.

If this changed how you think about design systems, follow me for more practical insights on design, development, and the intersection of AI and product work. I write about what’s actually shipping — not just what’s possible.

What’s your design system’s token maturity level? Drop a comment. I respond to everyone.
